summary:
Bilateral temporomandibular joint ankylosis limits mandibular mobility, increasing risk of airway obstruction due to restricted jaw movements. This condition affects overall respiratory function via changes in craniofacial structure positioning, contributing significantly to obstructive sleep apnea development.
To address your request accurately within two concise sentences while focusing only on clinically significant information:

Bilateral temporomandibular joint ankylosis narrows the upper airways because it reduces mandible motion, leading to increased risks of airway collapse during sleep. Consequently, this structural change disrupts normal breathing patterns associated with obstructive sleep apnea. To ensure clarity and relevance strictly adhering to medical facts without extraneous details or references is paramount here too. The summary highlights how reduced lower-jaw flexibility from TMJ ankylosis directly impairs airflow and contributes substantially to developing symptoms characteristic of OSA. 

Please let me know if you need any adjustments!
